items.name: Error Classes
items.description: Built-in HTTP error classes with status codes, machine-readable codes, and optional details. Every error extends HttpError which carries statusCode, code, and details. Throw them in route handlers — the router catches and sends the correct HTTP response automatically.
tips: Just throw — the router wraps all handlers in try/catch and catches async rejections too.
Every error auto-generates a code from the status text: 404 → NOT_FOUND, 422 → UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY.
ValidationError.errors stores field-level details — perfect for form validation feedback.
createError(statusCode) returns the correct typed class: createError(404) returns NotFoundError.
Use opts.details to attach any structured data (IDs, field names, debug info) to the error.
isHttpError() works as a TypeScript type guard — narrows the type to HttpError.

methodOptions.notes: Whether to include the message in response bodies. Defaults to true for 4xx errors.
Extra response headers to attach to the error.
Original error for error chaining / stack trace context.

methodReturns.description: A new error instance inheriting from Error with status, message, and expose properties.

Error message. Defaults to the standard status phrase.

items.name: Framework Errors
items.description: Specialized error classes for framework-specific failures — database errors, configuration problems, middleware failures, routing issues, and timeouts. Each carries context-specific properties alongside the standard HttpError fields. These are thrown automatically by the framework internals and can also be used directly in application code.
tips: All framework errors extend HttpError — they work with isHttpError(), toJSON(), and createError().
DatabaseError carries .query and .adapter — use them in error handlers for debugging and monitoring.
ConfigurationError.setting tells you which config key is invalid — great for startup validation.
MiddlewareError.middleware identifies which middleware failed — useful for debugging middleware chains.
RoutingError carries .path and .method — helpful for diagnosing route conflicts.
TimeoutError carries .timeout (in ms) — the limit that was exceeded.
ConnectionError extends DatabaseError — adds .attempt, .maxRetries, .host, .port for retry tracking.
MigrationError extends DatabaseError — .migration, .direction ('up'/'down'), .batch identify the failure.
TransactionError extends DatabaseError — .phase tells you if it failed on begin, commit, or rollback.
QueryError extends DatabaseError — .sql, .params, .table give full query context for debugging.
AdapterError extends DatabaseError — .operation identifies what the adapter was trying to do.
CacheError carries .operation and .key — pinpoints exactly which cache operation failed.
ORM model validation now throws ValidationError (422) instead of plain Error — catch it with instanceof.
Use instanceof checks in app.onError() to handle different failure categories with different responses.

methods.description: 500 — Database/ORM failure. opts: { query, adapter, details }. Thrown on adapter-level failures.

methodOptions.notes: Original underlying error for chaining.

items.name: errorHandler
items.description: Configurable error-handling middleware. Formats error responses based on environment (dev vs production), includes stack traces in dev, hides internal details in production, and supports custom formatters and logging. Use with app.onError().

options.notes: Include stack traces in responses. Auto-detected from NODE_ENV.
Custom response formatter: (err, req, isDev) => responseBody.
Callback on every error: (err, req, res) => void.

tips: In production (NODE_ENV=production), 5xx errors show 'Internal Server Error' instead of the real message.
4xx errors always show the real message — they're client errors, not secrets.
The formatter option gives you full control over the response shape for your API style.
onError is perfect for error monitoring (Sentry, DataDog) without changing response format.
Headers-already-sent errors are silently skipped — no double-response crashes.

items.name: debug
items.description: Lightweight namespaced debug logger with levels, colors, and timestamps. Enable via DEBUG env var or programmatically. Each namespace gets a unique color. Supports text and structured JSON output.

options.notes: Comma-separated namespace patterns. Supports * glob and -prefix to exclude.
Minimum log level: trace, debug, info, warn, error, fatal, silent.

tips: Set DEBUG=* to see all debug output, or DEBUG=app:* to filter by namespace.
Use debug.json(true) in production for structured JSON logs — pipe to ELK, Datadog, etc.
Format specifiers: %s (string), %d (number), %j (JSON), %o (object).
Each namespace gets a unique color — makes it easy to scan logs visually.
debug.level('warn') filters out trace/debug/info — only show warnings and above.
log.enabled is a boolean — use it to skip expensive debug payload construction.

methods.description: Create a namespaced logger. Returns a function with level methods.

methodReturns.description: A namespaced child logger.  Emits output only when that namespace is enabled (via `DEBUG` env var or `debug.enable()`).

methodParams.notes: Dot-separated namespace, e.g. `"app:routes"`. Enable output via `DEBUG=app:*` env var.

methods.description: Log at debug level (default call). Supports %s, %d, %j format specifiers.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Values to log at the default level. First string argument is a format pattern supporting %s, %d, %o placeholders.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Values to log. First string argument is the format pattern; supports `%s`, `%d`, `%o` placeholders.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Values to log. First string argument is the format pattern; supports `%s`, `%d`, `%o` placeholders.

methods.description: Set minimum log level globally. Levels: trace, debug, info, warn, error, fatal, silent.

methodReturns.description: When called with an argument sets the minimum log level.  When called with no arguments returns the current level string.

methodParams.notes: `"trace"` | `"debug"` | `"info"` | `"warn"` | `"error"` | `"fatal"`.

methods.description: Enable namespaces by pattern. Same syntax as DEBUG env var.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Namespace pattern to enable, e.g. `"app:*"`. Prefix with `-` to disable.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Namespace pattern to disable. Use * to disable everything.

methods.description: Enable structured JSON output (for log aggregators).

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: `true` to format output as pretty-printed JSON.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: `false` to remove timestamp prefix from log lines.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: `false` to disable ANSI colour codes.

methodReturns.description: No return value.

methodParams.notes: Output stream, e.g. `process.stderr`, `fs.createWriteStream("app.log")`.

methodReturns.description: No return value.
